name: The Ash Cough
historical_inspiration: Black Death (restructured feudal society, labor suddenly valuable), Plague of Justinian (weakened an empire at its peak), Columbian Exchange diseases (contact as vector). In our version: the disease feeds on fear-worship, creating a feedback loop — the more people dread it, the stronger the Ashfield god that spawned it becomes.
text:
It starts with ash in the lungs.

Traders who crossed the Ashfield arrive in Thornwall coughing ash-grey. Within a week, the cough spreads to the forge quarter. Within two weeks, the first child dies — not from the cough itself, but from the fear that follows. Her body is cold but her face is frozen in an expression of terror so complete that the woman who prepares the dead refuses to close her eyes.

The Ash Cough doesn't kill through the body. It kills through the mind. The infected cough ash — literal ash, ash-grey and fine — and as the disease progresses, they become afraid. Not of anything specific. Afraid of everything. Afraid of the sound of their own breathing. Afraid of sleep. Afraid of the faces of people they love.

The fear is the disease. The cough is just how it travels.

Scholars from the south send word: they've seen nothing like it. It doesn't respond to medicine. Bond-attuned healers can ease the symptoms, but the fear returns within hours. The only pattern anyone can identify: it started in the Ashfield. It's worst in people who were already anxious. And the ash — the ash that the infected cough — is identical to Ashfield volcanic dust.

The Forge Council is in emergency session. The southern trade quarter — if it exists — is calling for quarantine. The Rootwalkers have already changed their river route to avoid the region, abandoning three riversongs in the process. Knowledge that took generations to build, lost in a week because the alternative is a disease that turns your own mind against you.

And in the Ashfield, something is growing stronger. You can feel it at night — a pressure behind your temples, like something vast and newborn is learning to breathe by stealing yours.

choices.text: Focus on the healers. If Bond attunement eases it, pour everything into supporting them.
choices.consequence_text:
You organize the Bond-attuned into shifts. It's brutal work — each healer absorbs the patient's fear temporarily, carries it in their own chest for hours, then releases it through meditation. Some healers break. The fear is too much. They become patients themselves.

But the ones who hold — the ones who've learned to carry terror without drowning in it — they discover something. The fear has a frequency. A specific vibration, like a note in a song. And if you can match it — resonate with it instead of fighting it — you can drain it.

The Ash Cough doesn't disappear. But the death rate drops from one in three to one in twenty. You've found a treatment, not a cure. The disease is still fed by the Ashfield god's growing awareness. Treating the symptom doesn't stop the source.

But the children stop dying with terror on their faces. That's enough. For now.

choices.text: Go to the source. The Ashfield is breeding this. Someone needs to go there and find out what's happening.
choices.consequence_text:
The Ashfield is three days through dead ground that no one willingly walks. You walk it.

What you find at the center changes everything you understand about gods and worship. The volcanic vents have coalesced into a shape — not a body, not yet, but a suggestion of one. A face made of heat-shimmer. Hands made of ash currents. And around it, feeding it, a haze of dread so thick you can taste it on your tongue like metal.

The god doesn't see you. It doesn't have eyes yet. But it knows you're afraid, because everything near it is afraid, and your fear is food.

You learn the crucial thing: this god isn't causing the plague out of malice. It's breathing. The Ash Cough is its BREATH — the natural emission of a being made of fear. It doesn't know it's killing people. It doesn't know people exist. It's a newborn, and its crying is a plague.

You return with knowledge that makes the problem both simpler and more impossible: to stop the plague, you'd need to stop a god from breathing.

choices.text: Quarantine. Seal the mountain passes. Nobody in, nobody out. Survive this the way the Holds survive everything — by enduring.
choices.consequence_text:
The passes close. The trade route — if it existed — goes silent. Thornwall turns inward, as it always has, as the old masters always said it should.

The quarantine works, in the brutal way that quarantines work: the disease can't spread beyond the mountain. But it also can't leave. The people already infected are trapped with their fear, and the fear feeds the Ashfield god, and the god's breath makes more ash, and the cycle tightens like a fist.

People die. Fewer than without the quarantine — but the ones who die, die alone, because the quarantine means nobody goes to the infected quarter. The healers can't keep up. The healthy watch from behind sealed doors and feel something they'll never forgive themselves for: relief that it's not them.

Thornwall survives. The Iron Holds survive. But the trust between the forge quarter and the upper town — between the sick and the well — takes a generation to rebuild. Some walls, once built, don't come down when the crisis ends.

choices.text: Send word to the Rootwalkers. Their riversongs encode knowledge about the Ashfield that predates any settled memory.
choices.consequence_text:
The Rootwalkers don't want to help. Three of their riversongs — centuries of accumulated knowledge about the Ashfield crossing, the safe paths, the seasonal patterns — were abandoned when they rerouted to avoid the plague. Asking them to remember is asking them to reopen a wound.

But an elder comes. She's old enough to have learned the Ashfield songs from her grandmother, who learned them from hers. She sings them in the Forge Council hall — the first Rootwalker song ever heard in Thornwall — and the hall goes quiet, because the songs contain something nobody expected.

The Ashfield wasn't always feared. The oldest songs describe it as a place of renewal — where the earth shed its old skin and grew new. The fear is recent. Generations of travelers projecting dread onto a place they didn't understand, until the dread became a thing with a face.

The elder says: 'You built this god with your fear. Every traveler who cursed the Ashfield, every parent who told their child to stay away, every campfire story about the burning ground — you were praying. You just didn't know it.'

The solution, if there is one, isn't medical. It's theological. You need to change what people believe about the Ashfield. You need to unworship a god.

Nobody knows how to do that. But now, at least, you know it needs to be done.

cascade_notes: Each outcome branches the Ashfield Awakening threat differently. Treated = Stage 2 slowed, but not stopped. Investigated = knowledge unlocked for future runs. Quarantine = Thornwall fractures internally, trust damage. Rootwalker knowledge = the unworship path opens (unique endgame possibility). Players across different runs may reach different solutions, creating different worlds.
